results.possibleIdentity: Wade Zimmerman is a prominent New York City-based architectural photographer and designer whose career began in the late 1960s or early 1970s. In 1973, he was credited as a designer for the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) exhibition catalogue titled "Another Chance for Housing: Low-rise Alternatives." Throughout his long career, which continues into the 2020s, he has become one of the most prolific photographers of contemporary architecture, documenting works by internationally renowned architects such as Renzo Piano, David Adjaye, Christian de Portzamparc, SANAA, and Herzog & de Meuron. His work is frequently featured in major publications including Architectural Record, World Architecture, and ArchDaily. Zimmerman's photography often captures high-profile urban and technological projects, including the 1970 Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka (a landmark project by the Experiments in Art and Technology collaborative), the Second Avenue Subway in New York, and the Sugar Hill development in Harlem. As an artist, he has cited the work of Edward Hopper and Charles Sheeler as significant influences on his photographic style.

results.possibleIdentity: Barry Ross is a prolific American illustrator, painter, and author active since the late 1960s, whose work frequently bridged the gap between art, technology, and science. He is likely the individual identified as 'Barry Floss' in certain digitized records due to optical character recognition (OCR) errors in historical technical and hobbyist magazines (such as 'Practical Wireless' and 'Radio Communication' from the mid-1990s), which misidentified his authorship of the 'Hands-on Guide to Oscilloscopes' as 'Barry floss'. Ross is renowned for his technical and aviation illustrations, which appeared in 'Flying' magazine for over 30 years, as well as in science and technology journals and corporate reports for companies like RCA and Pratt & Whitney. His artistic practice involves creating paintings and drawings that explain the functions of complex machinery and systems for engineers, scientists, and the general public—an aim that directly mirrors the mission of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. In 1968, Ross was credited in copyright entries for technical works (such as general-purpose oscilloscope charts) produced for RCA, a corporation heavily involved in early E.A.T. projects like '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering'. Besides illustration, Ross is also a sculptor and a pilot.

results.possibleIdentity: Dr. Godfrey Stafford (1920–2013) was a prominent British physicist and the former director of the Rutherford Laboratory (now Rutherford Appleton Laboratory) from 1969 to 1981. While primarily known for his scientific career in particle physics and accelerator development, he was also an artist who exhibited his work regularly at the Royal Academy. His dual role as a high-level scientist and a practicing artist aligns with the spirit of the late 1960s and early 1970s art-science movements, such as Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which sought to bridge the gap between technical and creative disciplines.

Dr. Stafford Beer (Anthony Stafford Beer, 1926–2002) was a world-renowned British cyberneticist and management scientist who was deeply involved in the intersection of technology, systems theory, and art during the 1960s and 1970s. He is frequently cited alongside Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) in historical accounts of early media art, particularly due to his contributions to cybernetic theory and his participation in the influential 'Cybernetic Serendipity' exhibition at the Institute of Contemporary Arts in London (1968). Beer considered himself an artist as well as a scientist, practicing painting and poetry, and he designed the modernist 'Opsroom' for the Cybersyn Project in Chile (1971–1973), which is often discussed as a foundational work of systemic and technological design.

Lawrence (Larry) Stafford is an American painter associated with the Lyrical Abstraction movement, active during the late 1960s and early 1970s. His work, characterized by the use of industrial tools such as spray guns to apply paint to large canvases, was included in major exhibitions of the era, such as the 'Information' show at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) in 1970. He is documented in the Harry Shunk and Shunk-Kender photographic archive at the Getty Research Institute, a collection primarily dedicated to recording the activities of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and the New York avant-garde scene of that period. While he is an artist named Stafford active in the requested timeframe, he is not typically referred to with the title 'Dr.' in standard art historical texts.
